quotes.quote: I can't recognize my own incompetence
quotes.summary: Yegge announces js2-mode, a new JavaScript editing mode for Emacs that includes a full recursive-descent parser ported from Mozilla Rhino. The mode provides accurate syntax highlighting, async parsing, and bounce-style indentation. He details his failed attempt at incremental parsing and the surprisingly difficult problem of indentation, which he discovered is 'almost totally orthogonal to parsing.' The project is part of a larger effort to enable writing Emacs extensions in JavaScript instead of Emacs-Lisp.
quotes.key_insight: Building a full parser for syntax highlighting is straightforward compared to the nightmare of indentation, which requires handling an explosion of edge cases that parsers deliberately ignore.

quotes.quote: Going back to the XBox 360 or Playstation controllers feels totally rinky-dink, like I've traveled back to the 1980s and I'm using an Atari Joystick.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge reviews four console games he's played over the past year: Portal, Mario Galaxy, Zelda: Twilight Princess, and Uncharted: Drake's Fortune. He praises Portal's innovative gameplay and humor, calls Mario Galaxy possibly the best Mario since the original, admires Twilight Princess's emotional storytelling, and gushes over Uncharted's stunning visuals on his new PS3 and plasma TV. The post also includes his thoughts on console hardware preferences, particularly his enthusiasm for the Wii's innovative controls and his recent HD setup upgrade. Throughout, he maintains his characteristic conversational style while offering genuine recommendations for fellow gamers who share his taste in adventure and RPG titles.
quotes.key_insight: Great games justify entire console purchases, and the best ones create such immersive experiences that they engage all your senses and leave lasting emotional memories.

quotes.quote: The game is sumptuous, no better word for it.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge reviews four console games he's played over the past year: Portal, Mario Galaxy, Zelda: Twilight Princess, and Uncharted: Drake's Fortune. He praises Portal's innovative gameplay and humor, calls Mario Galaxy possibly the best Mario since the original, admires Twilight Princess's emotional storytelling, and gushes over Uncharted's stunning visuals on his new PS3 and plasma TV. The post also includes his thoughts on console hardware preferences, particularly his enthusiasm for the Wii's innovative controls and his recent HD setup upgrade. Throughout, he maintains his characteristic conversational style while offering genuine recommendations for fellow gamers who share his taste in adventure and RPG titles.
quotes.key_insight: Great games justify entire console purchases, and the best ones create such immersive experiences that they engage all your senses and leave lasting emotional memories.

quotes.quote: Everyone who applied had the same reasons for not applying as you do. And everyone here says: 'GOSH, I SURE AM HAPPY I CAME HERE!'
quotes.summary: Yegge provides a comprehensive guide to interviewing at Google, acknowledging upfront that his advice will upset people who don't know the topics he recommends studying. He explains the concept of the 'Interview Anti-Loop' - the reality that every employee has a set of coworkers who wouldn't have hired them, making interviews inherently random. The post covers both mental preparation (stay humble, don't question the interviewer's questions) and technical preparation (algorithms, data structures, graphs, Big-O, operating systems). He emphasizes that Google's high false-negative rate means rejection doesn't reflect your actual abilities, and encourages candidates to reapply after 6-12 months if they don't succeed initially.
quotes.key_insight: Tech interviews are inherently flawed and random due to interviewer bias and the 'Anti-Loop' phenomenon, so rejection doesn't reflect your abilities - prepare thoroughly, stay humble, and try again if you fail.

quotes.quote: If you're a n00b, you'll look at experienced code and say it's impenetrable, undisciplined crap written by someone who never learned the essentials of modern software engineering.
quotes.tone: provocative, irreverent, and deliberately contrarian with characteristic Yegge humor and self-deprecation
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that programmer maturity can be measured by their 'compression tolerance' - the ability to read and write dense code without excessive comments or metadata. He compares junior programmers' verbose commenting style to a 2-year-old's temporal narratives, both being attempts to make sense of complexity through storytelling. The post extends this metaphor to static typing, arguing that over-reliance on type systems and metadata modeling is another form of n00b behavior. He concludes that experienced programmers should resist the urge to model everything and instead focus on getting code written and making forward progress.
quotes.key_insight: Excessive commenting, static typing, and metadata modeling are all manifestations of the same insecurity - the n00b's need to tell themselves stories about code rather than just writing it.

quotes.quote: Static types tell a story about the computation, presumably to help both reader groups understand the intent of the program. But the static types can be thrown away at runtime, because in the end they're just stylized comments.

quotes.quote: Most Lisp introductions try to give you the 'Tao of Lisp', complete with incense-burning, chanting, yoga and all that stuff. What I really wanted in the beginning was a simple cookbook for doing my 'normal' stuff in Lisp.
quotes.summary: This post is a practical primer on Emacs Lisp aimed at programmers who know C, Java, or JavaScript but want to customize Emacs. Rather than teaching the 'Tao of Lisp' with philosophical abstractions, Yegge provides a cookbook-style reference mapping familiar programming constructs to their Elisp equivalents. The guide covers lexical elements, operators, control flow, functions, error handling, and even a basic object system using defstruct. Yegge acknowledges Elisp's quirks while arguing it's worth learning for the power it gives you over your editor.
quotes.key_insight: Learning Elisp doesn't require embracing Lisp philosophy—you can be productive by simply mapping familiar imperative programming patterns to their Lisp equivalents.

quotes.quote: I'm doing it on purpose.
quotes.summary: Yegge defends his notoriously long blog posts against critics who want shorter entries. He argues that his posts are intentionally essay-length (around 10 pages) because longer content has better 'survival characteristics' - greater reach and lasting impact. He presents a 'brain-cache theory' suggesting the ideal essay length should fill a reader's mental short-term cache and force ideas to be committed to long-term memory. This requires roughly one 'sitting' of 20-50 minutes, which is why his posts are calibrated to be exhausting but not crushing.
quotes.key_insight: Long-form writing succeeds not despite reader fatigue but because of it - exhausting the reader's mental short-term cache forces ideas into permanent memory.

quotes.quote: It's as if you're all in a pond, and I'm throwing a rock into it. Bigger rock, bigger splash.

quotes.quote: I believe that code weight wrecks projects and companies, that it forces rewrites after a certain size, and that smart teams will do everything in their power to keep their code base from becoming a mountain.
quotes.tone: opinionated, conversational, self-deprecating humor mixed with genuine frustration
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that code base size is the worst enemy of software development, a minority view he developed after creating a 500,000-line Java game that became unmanageable for a single developer. He contends that Java's static type system and lack of compression facilities (macros, lambdas, declarative structures) forces inevitable bloat, while industry 'solutions' like IDEs and design patterns actually make code bases larger rather than smaller. After years of evaluation, he concludes that switching to a more expressive JVM language like JavaScript (Rhino/ES4), Ruby, or Python could compress a well-engineered Java codebase by 50-75%. He predicts that while Java programmers remain in denial about the problem, alternative JVM languages will eventually become compelling replacements.
quotes.key_insight: The worst thing that can happen to a codebase is size, and Java's fundamental character makes bloat inevitable - the only solution is switching to more expressive languages that provide real compression facilities.

quotes.quote: Java is like a variant of the game of Tetris in which none of the pieces can fill gaps created by the other pieces, so all you can do is pile them up endlessly.

He contends that Java's static type system and lack of compression facilities (macros, lambdas, declarative structures) forces inevitable bloat, while industry 'solutions' like IDEs and design patterns actually make code bases larger rather than smaller. After years of evaluation, he concludes that switching to a more expressive JVM language like JavaScript (Rhino/ES4), Ruby, or Python could compress a well-engineered Java codebase by 50-75%. He predicts that while Java programmers remain in denial about the problem, alternative JVM languages will eventually become compelling replacements.
quotes.key_insight: The worst thing that can happen to a codebase is size, and Java's fundamental character makes bloat inevitable - the only solution is switching to more expressive languages that provide real compression facilities.

quotes.quote: People who don't habitually spit will expectorate up to thirty feet, like zoo camels, in order to bespittle you if you even suggest the possibility of using a Lisp or Scheme dialect at your company.

quotes.quote: I had an exciting morning of not getting fired today.
quotes.summary: Yegge writes a casual status update after a minor scare where an automated system disabled his Google account, leading Reddit to speculate he was being fired. He announces he's working on a book about Mozilla Rhino with Norris Boyd, building a JavaScript interpreter in Emacs-Lisp to create a world-class JavaScript IDE and allow writing Emacs extensions in JavaScript. He also weighs in on the ECMAScript 4 debate, obliquely criticizing Microsoft's opposition, and reaffirms his love for working at Google.
quotes.key_insight: JavaScript is poised to become a Next Big Language, and its elegance makes it a better choice than Emacs-Lisp for extending Emacs despite the unconventional implementation path.

quotes.quote: Resume screening is just pattern matching. People are trying to figure out if you have the skills they're looking for.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge provides brutally honest advice on writing technical resumes, drawing from his experience screening over 5,000 resumes and interviewing 1,200+ candidates. He argues that resumes should be stripped of personality, kept in plain text, and focused entirely on demonstrable skills. The post systematically dismantles common resume practices: weasel words that hide lack of contribution, wank words that inflate importance, certifications that signal weakness, and lies that always get caught. He emphasizes that resume screening is pure pattern matching—nobody cares about you as a person yet, they just want to assess your skills quickly.
quotes.key_insight: Technical resumes should be stripped down to verifiable facts about what you actually built and shipped, because everything else—personality, certifications, buzzwords, and exaggerations—actively works against you in the pattern-matching game of resume screening.

quotes.quote: "Utilize" has been scientifically demonstrated to be used only by stupid people, so if you use it you could easily be mistaken for one.

quotes.quote: Java programmers understand an average of seven fewer Computer Science concepts per hour spent with Java each day
quotes.summary: This satirical piece presents fake tech news stories that skewer various targets in the software industry. Yegge lampoons Java's verbosity and Eclipse's notorious startup time, takes shots at Ruby on Rails' scaling problems, mocks Microsoft's legacy code, and delivers a pointed commentary on the absurdity of using recursion questions in technical interviews. The piece is structured as a mock newsletter with increasingly absurd stories, culminating in juvenile humor about a vacuum cleaner.
quotes.key_insight: Tech industry sacred cows - from Java's enterprise patterns to algorithm-focused interviews - deserve relentless mockery because they've become rituals disconnected from actual software quality.

quotes.quote: Marketing. It's not the banner ads. It's what your friends are saying.
quotes.summary: Yegge recounts the chaotic behind-the-scenes story of how he came to give an OSCON keynote talk about marketing and branding. The piece weaves together his attendance at Foo Camp, where he witnessed OSI's futile attempt to protect the 'Open Source' trademark, with his last-minute preparation for a keynote he didn't expect anyone to attend. He discovers too late that he's the closing keynote speaker for 3,000 people, gets shepherded through a surreal backstage experience by a judgmental handler, and faces technical difficulties right as his talk begins. The post ends on a cliffhanger, promising the actual talk content in a follow-up.
quotes.key_insight: The open source movement's branding problem stems from failing to clearly define and legally protect what 'Open Source' actually means, leaving the term vulnerable to corporate co-optation.

quotes.quote: I am privileged to work at Google. It's a privilege. It's something that I have no expectations about.
quotes.summary: Yegge recounts his unexpectedly viral Foo Camp talk about porting Ruby on Rails to JavaScript running on the JVM via Mozilla Rhino. He explains Google's pragmatic language policy (C++, Java, Python, JavaScript only) and why this constraint, combined with his project's JVM requirements, led him to choose Rhino over Jython. The post details how they extended JavaScript with Ruby/Python-like methods and began a 20% project to add JavaScript 1.7 features to Rhino. He emphasizes that Google's clean codebase and engineering discipline enable this kind of experimentation within well-defined boundaries.
quotes.key_insight: Smart language constraints at scale aren't limiting—they enable experimentation by building deep expertise and reducing organizational friction.

quotes.quote: If you don't know how compilers work, then you don't know how computers work.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that compiler construction is the second most important computer science course because it integrates nearly everything else you learn in CS and equips you to solve real programming problems that constantly arise. He confesses to failing compilers the first time and only later understanding why they matter: compilers are everywhere, from syntax highlighting to code refactoring to DSLs. The post skewers programmers who avoid compilers, mocks the type-checking academic camp, and insists that writing your own compiler—despite becoming 'a lifetime of work'—is essential to truly understanding how computers work. Without this knowledge, you're condemned to the 'programmer B-list,' building bloated systems while waiting for someone else to fix your tools.
quotes.key_insight: Compiler knowledge is the gateway from being a tool user to being a tool maker—without it, you'll forever depend on others to solve the parsing and transformation problems that pervade all of programming.

quotes.quote: You can't fully understand how compilers work without knowing machine architecture, because compilers emit machine code.

quotes.summary: Yegge argues that compiler construction is the second most important computer science course because it integrates nearly everything else you learn in CS and equips you to solve real programming problems that constantly arise. He confesses to failing compilers the first time and only later understanding why they matter: compilers are everywhere, from syntax highlighting to code refactoring to DSLs. The post skewers programmers who avoid compilers, mocks the type-checking academic camp, and insists that writing your own compiler—despite becoming 'a lifetime of work'—is essential to truly understanding how computers work. Without this knowledge, you're condemned to the 'programmer B-list,' building bloated systems while waiting for someone else to fix your tools.
quotes.key_insight: Compiler knowledge is the gateway from being a tool user to being a tool maker—without it, you'll forever depend on others to solve the parsing and transformation problems that pervade all of programming.

quotes.quote: I was entrapped by a spell cast by an evil wizard, old friend, that's just me doing my best to explain something entirely outside my realm of experience.
quotes.summary: Yegge uses an elaborate fantasy tavern allegory to describe a toxic workplace situation, likely at a major tech company. The narrator and his friend Rauser are master builders who both fell under 'spells' cast by 'evil wizards' - manipulative political operators who exploit hardworking engineers. Rauser's story describes building a palace on a swamp under impossible deadlines, with constant schedule pressure, rumor campaigns, and the bizarre metaphor of being forced to eat endless marshmallows (busywork/dysfunction). The core message is that organizations of well-meaning 'sheep' are vulnerable to being devoured by political 'wolves' who operate invisibly until it's too late.
quotes.key_insight: Political operators in tech companies act like wolves among sheep - they exploit engineers' good faith and lack of political awareness to create toxic, unsustainable work environments while remaining invisible until catastrophic damage is done.

quotes.quote: You've heard about boiling frogs, of course, and I tell you: even if it's not true, that wives tale about frog-boiling has the ring of truth because things like it happen all the time.

quotes.quote: Google has a sort of event horizon, beyond which you're inevitably sucked in.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge shares diary entries from his first weeks as a Google employee ('Noogler') in 2005, describing the culture shock of abundant free food, the quirky perks like ball pits and massage chairs, and the exodus of Microsoft employees to Google's Kirkland office. The post humorously documents mishaps like losing his badge in a ball pit and escaping a boring tech talk, while painting a picture of Google as an engineering paradise where former Microsoft employees are thrilled to 'actually be writing code' again. The underlying message is that Google had become the gravitational center of tech talent, with an 'event horizon' that inevitably pulls in anyone who investigates it seriously.
quotes.key_insight: Google's combination of engineering autonomy, abundant perks, and freedom from bureaucracy created a talent black hole that was systematically draining other tech companies of their best engineers.

quotes.quote: If you want to spare yourself a lot of angst in deciding which programming language to use, then I recommend this simple rule: Go ugly early.
quotes.summary: Yegge predicts the imminent arrival of the 'Next Big Language' (NBL) and outlines the characteristics it must have to achieve mass adoption. He argues that language success requires corporate backing, C-like syntax (despite its flaws), dynamic typing with optional static types, strong performance, excellent tooling, and a comprehensive feature set. The post acknowledges that these requirements optimize for popularity rather than elegance, noting that the bar for 'not sucking' has become remarkably high. Yegge claims to know what NBL is but refuses to name it, instead providing enough hints for readers to deduce it themselves.
quotes.key_insight: Language adoption is determined not by elegance or technical superiority, but by meeting a high bar of practical requirements including familiar syntax, corporate backing, strong tooling, and comprehensive features - optimizing for programmer expectations rather than ideals.

quotes.quote: I can be like Paul Graham, and sit around in my underwear writing articles about how I get to sit around in my underwear writing articles about stuff, on account of having written everything in Lisp. Except I'll still have to wear pants.
quotes.summary: Yegge's friends stage an intervention about his notoriously long blog posts, convincing him to try writing shorter, columnist-style entries of about 800 words per week. He uses this as an opportunity to demonstrate Emacs Lisp programming by building a character-counting function from scratch. The post walks through creating an interactive Emacs command that counts non-whitespace characters, explaining concepts like save-excursion, let bindings, and the while loop. Despite promising brevity, the post about writing shorter posts ends up being nearly double his target length, a self-aware irony Yegge acknowledges at the end.
quotes.key_insight: Emacs's extensibility model, where editor commands and programming primitives are unified, creates a uniquely powerful environment that modern IDEs with their heavyweight plugin systems have failed to replicate.

quotes.quote: As you learn the editor commands, you're also learning how to program Emacs, because you can use all those commands in your elisp code.

quotes.quote: I think most software is crap. Well, that's not quite right. It's fairer to say that I think all software is crap.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that the fundamental problem with software is that we build 'dead' systems requiring constant reboots, rather than 'living' systems that can grow and evolve while running. He identifies key properties of great software—command shells, advice systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and introspection—all deriving from the principle that systems should never need to reboot. He critiques static type systems as tools for building inflexible 'hardware' rather than adaptable software, and suggests that truly great software exhibits an ineffable 'Quality Without a Name' (QWAN) that emerges from organic growth rather than rigid design.
quotes.key_insight: The best software systems are 'living' rather than 'dead'—designed to grow and evolve without rebooting—and this single principle drives all other qualities that make software great.

quotes.quote: Systems should never reboot. If you design a system so that it never needs to reboot, then you will eventually, even if it's by a very roundabout path, arrive at a system that will live forever.

quotes.quote: There's nothing wrong with static type systems. You just have to realize that when you use them, you're building hardware, not software.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that the fundamental problem with software is that we build 'dead' systems requiring constant reboots, rather than 'living' systems that can grow and evolve while running. He identifies key properties of great software—command shells, advice systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and introspection—all deriving from the principle that systems should never need to reboot. He critiques static type systems as tools for building inflexible 'hardware' rather than adaptable software, and suggests that truly great software exhibits an ineffable 'Quality Without a Name' (QWAN) that emerges from organic growth rather than rigid design.
quotes.key_insight: The best software systems are 'living' rather than 'dead'—designed to grow and evolve without rebooting—and this single principle drives all other qualities that make software great.

quotes.quote: T.S. needed a special rule for each possible way to break the orderly workings of the line, because otherwise people would argue endlessly. You had to show them that it was written down, a real rule, before they'd listen.
quotes.summary: This allegorical short story depicts a bureaucratic security guard (T.S.) who enforces rules with inflexible rigidity, refusing to help a desperate woman (Anushri) whose husband is critically injured. The guard is revealed to be a Java-based software system, complete with a terrible API and no unit tests. The crowd of passengers eventually solves the problem by collectively stepping out of line, allowing Anushri to be 'next' without breaking any rules. The story satirizes rigid software systems, bureaucratic thinking, and the absurdity of rules without exceptions, while celebrating human creativity in working around inflexible systems.
quotes.key_insight: Rigid, over-engineered systems with terrible APIs and no flexibility will always be routed around by humans who understand that sometimes the rules need to bend for compassion.

quotes.quote: People accuse me of being long-winded, but it's not often that breaking short wind, amusing as it can be, has any real long-term effect.
quotes.summary: Yegge returns after a blogging hiatus to reflect on his one-year blog anniversary and the phenomenon he calls 'Essay Molasses' - the slow, delayed spread of ideas that eventually reach critical mass. He then delivers a rambling critique of Agile methodology, comparing it to religious ceremony and arguing it's only useful for replicating derivative work, not creating anything truly innovative. The post concludes with his core philosophy: continuous self-improvement and becoming so valuable that you can 'call your own shots' rather than being subject to process-driven management.
quotes.key_insight: Long-form writing gives ideas the 'gestation period' they need to survive and spread, and continuous self-improvement makes you immune to process-cargo-cults like Agile.

quotes.quote: Most great software developers around the world don't use Agile. They just work hard, they stay lightweight, and they ship great stuff.
quotes.summary: Yegge follows up his controversial 'Good Agile Bad Agile' post by clarifying that Google's approach isn't the only alternative to Agile—most great software developers worldwide simply work hard and ship without any methodology. He argues that Agile is a niche practice amplified by loud marketing and consulting money, not a proven approach. The core of his argument is that Agile is technological superstition: like a chicken that learns to twist while playing piano, teams attribute success to their methodology when hard work is the actual cause. He warns that Agile's viral spread threatens to create workplace discrimination against non-believers, and closes with the revelation that 'Agile Manifesto' has exactly one two-word anagram: 'Egomania Itself.'
quotes.key_insight: Agile methodology is technological superstition—teams attribute their success to the methodology when hard work is the actual cause, just like a chicken that thinks its twisting dance produces food pellets.

quotes.quote: Agile Methodologies are for chumps.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that Agile methodologies are mostly a marketing scam preying on desperate developers, but there exists a 'good agile' exemplified by Google's engineering culture. He contrasts Bad Agile—with its rigid sprints, endless meetings, and date-driven development—against Google's incentive-based, autonomous approach where engineers choose their projects and launches emerge organically. The key insight is that great software comes from removing obstacles and aligning incentives rather than adding process. Google achieves this through exceptional perks, peer recognition, and a culture where gratitude drives performance better than any methodology.
quotes.key_insight: Great software emerges not from process and methodology, but from creating an environment where launching is the natural outcome—through aligned incentives, autonomy, and genuine care for engineers.

quotes.quote: Liking and using are mostly orthogonal dimensions, and if you like the language you're using even a little bit, you're lucky.
quotes.summary: Yegge observes Ruby's rapid rise in bookstore shelf space to rival Python, then pivots to his central thesis: Java's biggest failing is its lack of literal syntax for data structures. While Java's platform (JVM, tooling, concurrency libraries) remains excellent, the language forces programmers into verbose, Swing-style object assembly patterns for hashmaps, lists, and nested structures that other languages handle with simple literal declarations. This verbosity drives Java developers toward XML and external data formats, adding impedance mismatch and performance overhead. He concludes that Ruby's success stems from better complexity management, and Sun's move to support scripting languages on the JVM may be the right approach since Java prioritizes performance and reliability over productivity.
quotes.key_insight: A language's productivity ceiling is determined by its syntax and type system design decisions, not its libraries - and Java's lack of literal syntax for common data structures forces developers into verbose patterns that scripting languages solve elegantly.

quotes.quote: I have a lot of trouble writing about Ruby, because I find there's nothing to say. It's why I almost never post to the O'Reilly Ruby blog. Ruby seems so self-explanatory to me.

quotes.key_insight: A language's productivity ceiling is determined by its syntax and type system design decisions, not its libraries - and Java's lack of literal syntax for common data structures forces developers into verbose patterns that scripting languages solve elegantly.

quotes.quote: JavaScript is probably the most important language in the world today. Funny, huh? You'd think it would be Java or C++ or something.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge argues that JavaScript is unexpectedly the most important programming language in the world because it's the gateway to browser-based applications, which is where most users now live. Despite JavaScript's flaws and the nightmarish complexity of browser programming (with its multiple languages, ugly APIs, and Microsoft's deliberate standards sabotage), the economics of web development make it inevitable that a Rails-like simplification will emerge for the browser. Yegge also reflects on blogger's block, realizing that the pressure of public criticism was stifling his creativity, and decides to stop reading comments to recapture the creative freedom of falling-asleep thinking.
quotes.key_insight: Despite being universally despised by 'real' programmers, JavaScript's captive browser audience makes it the most strategically important language, and the economic pressure for simpler web development will inevitably produce a transformative framework.

quotes.quote: Microsoft has always been awful at making APIs, always always always, and I've decided over the years to credit this to malice rather than incompetence.

quotes.quote: I can tell you this much: that girl cost us thousands of dollars over the next 18 months.
quotes.summary: Yegge recounts his unexpected journey into anime fandom, sparked by watching Miyazaki's Spirited Away with his wife. A chance encounter with a knowledgeable but poor Suncoast employee named Ashley led to discovering Twelve Kingdoms and Last Exile, which became obsessive favorites. After spending thousands on DVDs and rewatching series multiple times, he shares his curated top 10 list of anime series and movies, emphasizing that while 90% is crap, the good stuff is genuinely fantastic. The post serves as both a personal narrative and a recommendation guide for anime newcomers.
quotes.key_insight: Great art exists in every medium, but finding it requires patience, trusted recommendations, and the willingness to persist through initial unfamiliarity.

quotes.quote: You know how little kids at a certain age like to watch the same movie over and over and over again, for up to a year, and child psychologists say that each time they see it they're seeing it from a new perspective? Well, 12 Kingdoms was like that for us.

quotes.quote: RoR is like having a pillow surgically removed from your face. I can breathe again.
quotes.summary: Yegge explains his month-long blogging hiatus, attributing it to a combination of factors: an unkept promise about a controversial technical topic, a work project, a vacation spent building a Ruby on Rails site, and most significantly, the psychological pressure of sudden popularity after Joel Spolsky linked to his blog. He describes the anxiety of having 70,000 readers scrutinizing his work and announces a strategy of writing shorter posts to overcome the block.
quotes.key_insight: Blogging success creates a paradox where increased audience size generates psychological pressure that makes authentic writing more difficult, not less.

quotes.quote: Your body is no longer a prison for your soul. It's become more like a house, one that you can decorate to your tastes. In the fullness of time it may even become more like clothes for your soul, and you'll change it daily.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that cosmetic body modification will eventually become as socially acceptable as changing clothes, but American puritanical culture currently forces people to lie about procedures they've had. He catalogs the strange inconsistencies in what's taboo (nose jobs, liposuction) versus acceptable (hair coloring, tattoos), suggesting economics will eventually drive full acceptance since attractive people receive better treatment. The essay is actually a meta-commentary on how difficult it is to introduce radical new ideas to large groups, using plastic surgery acceptance as a teaching metaphor for upcoming technical posts.
quotes.key_insight: The real subject isn't plastic surgery but the meta-problem of how group membership and cultural inertia make people resist obviously beneficial new ideas, whether cosmetic or technical.

quotes.quote: When you put two people together, they're smarter than one person. But a group of three or four is back to being about as smart as a single person. A group of ten to twenty people acts about as smart as a lost child, and a group of fifty is only about as smart as a dog.

quotes.quote: So what makes a programmer famous? Apparently not programming! Or at least not their actual code.
quotes.summary: Yegge reflects on being included in a 'top 10 programmers' list despite not considering himself exceptional, concluding that fame in programming comes from writing frameworks people love or writing prose about programming—not from actual code quality. He observes that most programmers we admire are known for building extensible systems or authoring compelling technical writing, since their actual code is rarely examined and usually looks ordinary. He extends this to wealth, noting that rich programmers got that way through startups and business acumen, not coding excellence. The post is a self-deprecating meditation on what actually matters in a programming career versus what we think matters.
quotes.key_insight: Fame and wealth in programming come not from writing great code, but from shipping useful frameworks and writing compelling prose about the craft.

quotes.quote: I'd love it even more if I were a fat old dragon sitting on a big pile of gold, because then I could buy a zillion-inch monitor and work on programs I like.
quotes.summary: Yegge reflects on being included in a 'top 10 programmers' list despite not considering himself exceptional, concluding that fame in programming comes from writing frameworks people love or writing prose about programming—not from actual code quality. He observes that most programmers we admire are known for building extensible systems or authoring compelling technical writing, since their actual code is rarely examined and usually looks ordinary. He extends this to wealth, noting that rich programmers got that way through startups and business acumen, not coding excellence. The post is a self-deprecating meditation on what actually matters in a programming career versus what we think matters.
quotes.key_insight: Fame and wealth in programming come not from writing great code, but from shipping useful frameworks and writing compelling prose about the craft.

quotes.quote: Some Ph.D.s can, but how many of them is it, really? From an industry perspective, an alarming number of them are no-ops.
quotes.summary: Yegge imagines a future where 'Wizard Schools' — elite seven-year boarding schools for programming prodigies — have revolutionized tech education and made traditional CS degrees nearly obsolete. Written as a retrospective from 2017, the fictional narrator describes how these Harry Potter-themed academies produce graduates who type 160 WPM, code in 'folding languages' (Lisp/Haskell derivatives), can draw, speak multiple languages, and command salaries of $250K-$1M. The post is actually a critique of current CS education and a manifesto for what programming education could become if it prioritized practical coding skills, physical fitness, arts training, and hiring brilliant industry practitioners as teachers.
quotes.key_insight: CS education is fundamentally broken because universities prioritize research over teaching practical programming skills, and someone who built an intensive, practice-focused alternative could revolutionize the industry.

quotes.quote: What matters is what you *know*, and what you can *do*, and a Ph.D. laureate in CS these days, even from a 'top' university, has about an 80% chance of failing interviews at one of these companies.

quotes.quote: Even if you do most of your coding in the comfy confines of a visual IDE, it can be awfully handy to keep Emacs around for your fine-grained text surgery.
quotes.summary: Yegge enthusiastically reviews Emacs 22's new features, focusing primarily on two game-changers: native Unicode/UTF-8 support and the ability to use Lisp expressions in replacement strings for search-and-replace operations. Through progressively complex examples, he demonstrates how the enhanced replace-regexp command transforms text manipulation from tedious manual work into powerful one-liner transformations. He concludes with advice on learning Emacs Lisp, arguing it's a worthwhile investment that won't become obsolete and recommending specific books for mastering it.
quotes.key_insight: The combination of regular expressions and inline Lisp evaluation in Emacs 22's replace-regexp creates a text transformation power tool that surpasses specialized refactoring IDEs for many real-world editing tasks.

quotes.quote: If you want to manage badly enough, then you will manage, badly enough.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that the desire to become a manager is itself a red flag, as the best managers are actually leaders who never sought management roles. He contends that empathy is the single most important trait for managing developers, capable of smoothing over nearly any mistake. The post criticizes companies that treat management as a promotion over engineering, arguing this drives away talent and pushes organizations toward mediocrity. He distinguishes sharply between management (bureaucratic simulation of leadership) and actual leadership (vision-driven inspiration), suggesting that great engineers will build great things faster without managers getting in their way.
quotes.key_insight: The catch-22 of software management is that those who want it most are usually worst at it, because true leadership comes from vision and empathy rather than desire for authority.

quotes.quote: You should never give power to someone who craves it, for reasons that I hope are obvious.

quotes.quote: The game is so damned addictive that when you're not playing, you wind up looking outside and thinking 'man, that lighting model is really realistic!' before you remember you're not actually playing at that particular moment.
quotes.summary: Yegge reviews The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion, a massively addictive single-player RPG that he's been playing instead of blogging. Despite frequent crashes (which he attributes to C++), ugly NPC faces, and repetitive voice acting, he finds the game exceptional due to its beautiful scenery, smart monster AI, and immersive single-player experience. He argues that single-player games have distinct advantages over MMORPGs because they're focused entirely on the player and don't require dealing with toxic multiplayer communities or subscription fees.
quotes.key_insight: Even deeply flawed games can be incredibly compelling when they focus on making the player the hero of their own story, which is something multiplayer games fundamentally cannot do.

quotes.quote: I've been dead ever since then, you know. I didn't close my eyes for a single night, for four years after he died, without thinking of offing myself, because I missed him so much.
quotes.summary: This deeply personal essay is a departure from Yegge's usual technical blogging, centered on his brother Dave who died of lymphoma at age 23 after being misdiagnosed. Yegge reflects on how Dave taught him to write, to have a sense of humor, and to live fully. He reveals that for four years after Dave's death, he thought about suicide every night. The post serves as both an elegy and an explanation for why Yegge writes—he's writing for Dave. He announces his intention to broaden his blog beyond technical topics, while acknowledging readers' expectations, and asks that this particular post not be shared on Reddit or Digg.
quotes.key_insight: Living fully and making people laugh matters more than anything you could ever write about, because you never know how long it'll last.

quotes.quote: It was philosophers that got us out of that Dark Ages mess, and no small number of them lost their lives in doing so. And today, the philosophy majors are the butts of the most jokes, because after the philosophers succeeded in opening our minds, we forgot why we needed them.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that the software industry is trapped in religious-like tribalism around programming languages, where rational discourse is impossible because developers treat their preferred languages as sacred objects. He draws an extended parallel between programming language communities and organized religion, complete with popes, hierarchies, and the persecution of heretics. The essay was prompted by hostile reactions to his previous criticism of Lisp, which he uses as evidence that even supposedly enlightened technical communities are capable of the same irrational zealotry that characterized the Dark Ages. He calls for philosopher-like thinkers to emerge in software who can challenge cherished beliefs and move the industry forward through rational inquiry rather than tribal warfare.
quotes.key_insight: The software industry's religious attachment to programming languages prevents the rational discourse needed for genuine progress, and we need philosopher-like critics willing to challenge sacred cows despite the inevitable backlash.

quotes.quote: If Lisp were acceptable, then we'd all be using it.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that despite Lisp's legendary status and theoretical elegance, no current implementation is actually acceptable for practical use. He identifies four major problems: fragmentation across incompatible dialects (Scheme vs Common Lisp vs various implementations), an outdated specification lacking modern features like threading and Unicode, CLOS being a bolted-on object system that doesn't achieve true OOP seamlessness, and macros that while powerful are poorly supported by tools and remain second-class citizens. He notes that Paul Graham's Arc vaporware has paradoxically hurt Lisp adoption by making programmers wait rather than commit to existing options. Yegge concludes that Lisp needs a revolution with a benevolent dictator, a new name, and lessons learned from 45 years of language evolution.
quotes.key_insight: Lisp's theoretical elegance is undermined by practical fragmentation, outdated specifications, and bolted-on systems that prevent it from ever achieving the seamless experience of modern languages.

quotes.quote: In Javaland, by King Java's royal decree, Verbs are owned by Nouns.
quotes.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Java's rigid object-oriented paradigm forces programmers to wrap every action (verb) inside a class (noun), leading to absurdly verbose code with unnecessary abstractions like AbstractProxyMediator and NotificationStrategyFactory. Using an extended allegory of 'Kingdom of Javaland,' he illustrates how this noun-centric thinking distorts natural problem-solving by requiring chaperone objects for simple operations. He contrasts Java with functional languages and even C++, which allow standalone functions as first-class citizens. The post concludes with a call for Java to add first-class functions so programmers can think and code more naturally.
quotes.key_insight: Java's insistence that every function must belong to a class forces programmers to create absurd noun hierarchies that obscure simple actions, when first-class functions would let code mirror natural thought.

quotes.quote: To me, mainstream languages have failed because they're still fundamentally serial, fundamentally early von Neumann, fundamentally single-box languages.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that Moore's Law represents a dead end because we're optimizing serial computation on fundamentally flawed von Neumann architecture instead of pursuing parallel computing. He contends that programming languages have all failed because they're designed for outdated hardware, and that programmers' resistance to learning new paradigms keeps us trapped in this evolutionary dead-end. The solution requires simultaneously revolutionizing hardware, languages, data structures, and algorithms—but programmers' aversion to painful learning makes this unlikely. He concludes by encouraging continuous learning despite the pain, as the only path toward potential improvement.
quotes.key_insight: The entire computing stack—hardware, languages, algorithms—must change simultaneously to escape our von Neumann dead-end, but programmers' refusal to endure the pain of learning new paradigms keeps us trapped.

quotes.quote: If you want a job at a company like Microsoft, Yahoo!, Apple, or Amazon.com, they're going to have high standards. It doesn't matter if you 'know how to program'.
quotes.summary: Yegge responds to accusations of being a snobbish interviewer by drawing satirical parallels to other demanding professions like airline pilots and brain surgeons. He argues that top tech companies have legitimately high standards because they've been burned by lowering them, and that software engineering has fragmented into specialized roles just like medicine. The post acknowledges interviewing is imperfect but defends the practice of testing candidates on computer science fundamentals, while advising candidates to research companies, vote with their feet, and improve the process from within rather than complaining anonymously online.
quotes.key_insight: Technical interviewing's high standards exist because software engineering is a demanding profession with real consequences for failure, and rather than complaining about it, candidates should either acquire the skills companies need or find companies whose priorities match their own.

quotes.quote: But when they lower their standards, they get burned. The 747 crashes, the patient dies, the juggler drops a bowling pin on someone's head, the tiger rips someone's throat out.

quotes.quote: If you don't know something, don't go berserk on us. Just go read about it on Wikipedia. It's not as hard as you think!
quotes.summary: Yegge responds to accusations of being a snobbish interviewer by drawing satirical parallels to other demanding professions like airline pilots and brain surgeons. He argues that top tech companies have legitimately high standards because they've been burned by lowering them, and that software engineering has fragmented into specialized roles just like medicine. The post acknowledges interviewing is imperfect but defends the practice of testing candidates on computer science fundamentals, while advising candidates to research companies, vote with their feet, and improve the process from within rather than complaining anonymously online.
quotes.key_insight: Technical interviewing's high standards exist because software engineering is a demanding profession with real consequences for failure, and rather than complaining about it, candidates should either acquire the skills companies need or find companies whose priorities match their own.

quotes.quote: You can be a good, solid, professional programmer without knowing much math. But hey, you don't really need to know how to program, either.
quotes.summary: Yegge argues that programmers should learn math, but not the math taught in schools. After spending 15 months relearning mathematics, he discovered that the curriculum (calculus, trig, geometry) is poorly suited for programmers, who actually need discrete math, probability, statistics, and linear algebra. He advocates for a breadth-first, Wikipedia-surfing approach to learning math rather than grinding through exercises, emphasizing that programming experience makes math much easier to pick up.
quotes.key_insight: Programmers should learn math breadth-first by surveying the field and learning what tools exist, because problem identification matters more than memorizing formulas—and programming experience makes mathematical concepts surprisingly accessible.

quotes.quote: Math is a lot easier to pick up after you know how to program. In fact, if you're a halfway decent programmer, you'll find it's almost a snap.

quotes.quote: Blog entries should be organized by popularity, not time. Or ideally, you can pick either one. Why the hell don't blogs do that?
quotes.summary: Yegge explains why he's reluctantly starting a blog despite hating the blog format. He critiques blogs for their chronological ordering, lack of threaded and inline comments, no version history, and poor content editing tools. After considering building his own solution in Ruby on Rails but realizing it would be another multi-year project like his 7-year game development effort, he pragmatically chooses Blogger. The post serves as both a manifesto for better self-publishing software and an explanation for why he's settling for imperfect tools.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between our vision for how software should work and our ability to build it forces painful compromises, especially when ideas far outnumber available years.

quotes.quote: The commentary on a blog entry should grow outward, not downward.
